currentUse: Church and school campus sold by the Archdiocese of Chicago in September 2025 to an expanding church congregation (identified as "The Chicago Church" in the Traded deal headline), which is preserving the property's use as a community/worship institution. Prior to sale it was a shuttered secondary worship site of St. Procopius Parish, used occasionally for weddings, funerals and wakes.
summary: Providence of God (Dievo Apvaizdos), 717 W. 18th St. in Pilsen, still stands. The parish was canonically suppressed and united to St. Procopius Parish by decree of Archbishop Blase Cupich given May 23, 2016, effective July 1, 2016, as part of the consolidation of Pilsen's six parishes into three, citing demographic change, a 29% ten-year decline in Sunday Mass attendance, fewer priests and unpaid parish debts. The church continued as a worship site until regular services ended in summer 2017 despite parishioner opposition; St. Procopius thereafter allowed the building to be used for weddings, funerals and wakes. In September 2025 the Archdiocese of Chicago sold the two-building campus (26,365 sq ft church plus 14,259 sq ft school at 1814 S. Union Ave. and 717 W. 18th St., 40,623 sq ft total) for $1,850,000 through SVN Chicago Commercial to an expanding church congregation described as preserving the property's institutional use; the Traded deal headline names the buyer as The Chicago Church. The building itself, a 1927 twin-steeple Romanesque church, had by the 1960s become home to a largely Mexican-American congregation that hosted one of the city's largest Via Crucis processions, and its loss was described by displaced parishioners as devastating. St. Procopius still styles itself "St. Procopius/Providence of God," carrying the suppressed parish's name forward.

developments.headline: Parish canonically suppressed and united to St. Procopius
developments.detail: Archbishop Blase Cupich's decree (given May 23, 2016, effective July 1, 2016) suppressed Providence of God Parish and united it to St. Procopius Parish, following a Pilsen planning process begun in early 2015 under Bishop Alberto Rojas that recommended reducing Pilsen from six parishes to three. The decree states the parish was established in 1903 as a personal parish for Lithuanian-speaking Catholics and became territorial in 1959; it kept the church as a sacred building for divine worship (canon 1214), open for baptisms and matrimony, and commended sacramental records to the Bernardin Archives.

sources.title: Decree: Suppression of Providence of God Parish and its Union with St. Procopius Parish
sources.publisher: Archdiocese of Chicago (Office of the Archbishop)

developments.headline: Regular services end at Providence of God
developments.detail: Despite parishioner opposition, regular church services at Providence of God ended in summer 2017. St. Procopius allowed the building to remain in use for special occasions including weddings, funerals and wakes. Former parishioners told Block Club Chicago the closure dispersed active parish groups (Guadalupanos, Grupo de la Virgen de la Luz, Nuevo Renacer) and that many older parishioners never settled at St. Procopius.

sources.title: In Pilsen, Churches Are More Than Sunday Mass — And Their Closures Are 'Devastating'

developments.headline: Archdiocese sells the campus for $1.85 million to another church
developments.detail: SVN Chicago Commercial's Cawthon-Labriola Group brokered the sale of the former Providence of God campus at 1814 S. Union Ave. and 717 W. 18th St.: a 40,623 sq ft two-building campus (26,365 sq ft two-level church, 14,259 sq ft three-level school, ~15-car lot) on 29,475 sq ft of land, sold for $1,850,000 to an expanding church congregation described as preserving the property's use as a long-standing community institution. The Traded deal headline identifies the buyer as The Chicago Church purchasing from the Archdiocese of Chicago. An earlier SVN listing marketing the site as a 4-building campus (adding convent and rectory) is now inactive.

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: Three refinements, no hard contradictions. (1) Founding date: archive says 1900; the archdiocese's own 2016 suppression decree states the parish "was established in 1903 as a personal parish for Lithuanian speaking Catholics," while South Side Weekly says established 1900 as Chicago's second Lithuanian parish — likely a congregation-organized-1900 vs. canonically-erected-1903 discrepancy; both dates appear in credible sources. (2) Closure: "closed 2016" is correct for the canonical suppression (decree given May 23, 2016, effective July 1, 2016), but the church itself remained an active worship site until summer 2017 and remained a consecrated sacred building available for weddings/funerals/wakes for years after; the property was not sold until September 2025. (3) "Under Renew My Church": the suppression decree does not mention Renew My Church — it resulted from a Pilsen-specific planning process begun in early 2015 under Bishop Alberto Rojas, which predates the Renew My Church rollout; press coverage treats it as the 2016 six-into-three Pilsen consolidation. The flagged 2023 source tension may stem from the 1900/1903 date or from the building remaining in occasional use long after "closure."
gaps: Exact closing date of the 2025 sale not verified from an opened page (a search summary indicated September 19, 2025, but the REJournals article I opened, dated 2025-09-24, does not state the closing date). Buyer identity "The Chicago Church" rests on the Traded deal headline; the traded.co and Connect CRE pages returned 403 and I could not open them to confirm details or learn what denomination/plans the buyer has — no independent press profile of the buyer found. No decree formally relegating the church building to profane (non-worship) use was locatable (archchicago.org blocks fetching; a 2016-era news release about such decrees exists but its content was inaccessible), so the canonical status of the building at time of sale is unconfirmed. The fate of the convent and rectory (included in the earlier 4-building SVN listing but not in the 2-building sale reported by REJournals) is unclear. Searches in Lithuanian (Dievo Apvaizdos parapija Čikaga) and English for 2024-2026 found no Lithuanian-community reunions, preservation campaigns, or commemorations specific to this building beyond Draugas heritage-guide references; a 2016-era Change.org petition to keep the church open exists but was not opened. Current interior condition and disposition of Lithuanian artifacts unknown.
